chunks.heading: Your brain runs on autopilot
chunks.idea: Most of what you 'decide' each day was never really decided. A fast, silent part of your mind answers for you before the thinking part even wakes up.
chunks.detail:
Think about your morning. You didn't calculate how to hold your toothbrush or plan each step to the kitchen. It just happened, smooth and automatic.

That smoothness is a system. Your brain has a fast lane that handles almost everything without asking you first. It reads faces, dodges a ball, finishes the phrase 'bread and ___' — instantly, with no effort.

There's also a slow lane. It's the part that does 17 x 24, or weighs a big choice. It's careful, but it's lazy and tires quickly, so your brain avoids using it whenever it can.

Here's the catch. The fast lane is guessing. Most of the time its guesses are great, which is why you trust it. But it uses shortcuts, and shortcuts can be quietly, confidently wrong.

So when we say 'your brain lies to you', we don't mean you're stupid. We mean your fast lane hands you an answer that *feels* true — and you rarely stop to check it.
chunks.example: Try this: 'A bat and a ball cost $1.10 together. The bat costs $1 more than the ball. How much is the ball?' Almost everyone instantly feels the answer is 10 cents. It isn't — it's 5 cents. Your fast lane blurted '10' before your slow lane checked the math.
points: Two mental gears: a fast, automatic one and a slow, effortful one
The fast gear runs almost everything and feels effortless
The slow gear is accurate but lazy — you avoid it
'Bad thinking' is usually the fast gear guessing without a check
chunks.key_takeaway: Most of your thinking is a fast, automatic guess that feels like certain truth.

key_terms.definition: The fast, automatic, effortless part of your mind that reacts before you consciously think.
The slow, deliberate part of your mind that does careful reasoning — and gets tired quickly.

chunks.heading: A bias is a bug that ships in every brain
chunks.idea: A cognitive bias isn't a rare glitch or a sign you're gullible. It's a wiring pattern that comes standard in every human head — including the smartest ones.
chunks.detail:
The word 'bias' sounds like an accusation. It isn't. Here it just means your mind leans a certain way, on its own, without you choosing to.

A bias is different from being uninformed. If you don't know a fact, learning it fixes the gap. A bias stays even when you know better — it's baked into how the machine runs.

And it's not random. Biases are predictable. Show the same trick to a thousand people and most of them slip the same way, in the same direction. Scientists can literally forecast the mistake.

That's the strange, useful part. Because these errors are systematic, they can be studied, named, and spotted. A random mistake you can only shrug at. A predictable one you can learn to catch.

Smart doesn't save you. Experts fall for these too — sometimes harder, because they're so sure of themselves. The goal isn't to feel dumb; it's to know where the potholes are.
chunks.example: A famous study found that dividing 2000 by half and adding 30 makes many people confidently say '1030'. Halving means dividing by 2, so 2000 ÷ 0.5 is actually 4000, giving 4030. The pull toward the wrong answer is so consistent that researchers expect it.
points: A bias is a built-in leaning, not missing information
Knowing the fact often doesn't stop the bias
Biases are predictable — same trap, same slip, across people
Intelligence doesn't make you immune; overconfidence can make it worse
chunks.key_takeaway: A cognitive bias is a standard, predictable flaw in everyone's thinking — not a personal failing.

key_terms.definition: A consistent, built-in pattern where your mind drifts away from clear reasoning.
A mistake that repeats in the same direction, so it can be predicted rather than being random.

chunks.heading: Shortcuts: fast, cheap, and sometimes wrong
chunks.idea: Your brain doesn't have time to weigh everything, so it swaps hard questions for easy ones without telling you. These swaps are called heuristics — and they're the source of most biases.
chunks.detail:
The world throws too much at you. You can't run the numbers on every choice, so your mind uses rules of thumb — quick tricks that usually work.

One big trick: if an example jumps to mind easily, your brain assumes it must be common. Easy to recall feels like 'happens a lot'. That's the availability shortcut.

Another: your brain quietly rewrites a hard question into an easier one. 'Is this a good investment?' silently becomes 'Do I like this company?' — and answers *that* instead.

Most of the time these shortcuts are a gift. They let you act fast and be right often, without frying your brain on every tiny decision.

But a shortcut is a trade. You buy speed by giving up accuracy. When the easy answer and the true answer part ways, the shortcut quietly hands you the wrong one — and it still feels right.
chunks.example: After seeing news coverage of a plane crash, many people feel flying is dangerous and choose to drive instead. But driving is far riskier per mile. The crash was just easier to *picture*, so the brain rated it as more likely — a textbook availability slip.
points: Heuristics are mental shortcuts that trade accuracy for speed
Availability: if it's easy to recall, you assume it's common
Substitution: your brain answers an easier question than the one asked
Shortcuts are usually helpful — biases are their side effects
chunks.key_takeaway: Biases are the price you pay for mental shortcuts that are usually worth it.

key_terms.definition: A quick rule of thumb your brain uses to answer fast instead of reasoning it all out.
Judging how likely something is by how easily an example comes to mind.

chunks.heading: Evolution built the bugs on purpose
chunks.idea: Your biases aren't accidents — they're features left over from staying alive. A brain that jumped to conclusions often out-survived a brain that paused to think.
chunks.detail:
Picture an ancestor on the savanna. The grass rustles. One person freezes and assumes 'lion'. Another calmly gathers evidence before reacting.

Most times, it's just wind. The cautious thinker is 'right' more often. But the one time it really is a lion, they're lunch — and the jumpy one lives to have kids.

So evolution didn't aim for *accurate*. It aimed for *survived and reproduced*. When being wrong in one direction is cheap and being wrong in the other is deadly, the brain learns to lean toward the cheap mistake.

That's why we're wired for fast fear, quick trust of our own group, and snap judgments. Those instincts kept our ancestors breathing long enough to become us.

The twist: you now live in a world of spreadsheets, headlines, and strangers online — not lions. The old wiring still fires, but it's often solving the wrong problem.
chunks.example: This is called error management: like a smoke alarm set to be too sensitive. It shrieks at burnt toast a hundred times, which is annoying but harmless — because missing the one real fire once would be fatal. Your fear instinct is tuned the same way: cry wolf cheaply to never miss the real wolf.
points: Evolution optimized for survival, not for being correct
When one error is deadly and the other is cheap, the brain leans toward the cheap one
Old instincts (fast fear, group loyalty) once kept us alive
Those same instincts misfire in a modern world of data and strangers
chunks.key_takeaway: Your biases are ancient survival tools running in a world they were never designed for.

key_terms.definition: The idea that the brain is tuned to make the less costly kind of mistake, even if it errs more often.
When instincts built for an old environment misfire in the modern one they weren't designed for.

chunks.heading: The one that fools everyone: confirmation bias
chunks.idea: Once you believe something, your brain stops being a judge and becomes a lawyer — hunting for evidence you're right and waving away everything that says you're wrong.
chunks.detail:
Notice what your mind does with a belief you hold. A fact that agrees with you slides right in, easy and welcome. A fact that disagrees gets poked, doubted, explained away.

That's confirmation bias. You're not weighing both sides. You've quietly picked a side, and now you're collecting ammo for it.

It's sneaky because it feels like research. You *think* you're being open-minded and gathering evidence. Really you're just shopping for reasons you already had.

The internet pours fuel on this. Search engines and feeds show you more of what you click, so your beliefs echo back at you louder and louder, feeling more 'obvious' each time.

The fix is uncomfortable but simple: go looking for the strongest case *against* what you believe. Not the weakest, easy-to-mock version — the best one. If your idea survives that, it's probably worth keeping.
chunks.example: Imagine you're sure a coworker dislikes you. You'll notice every time they don't say hi and read it as proof — while ignoring the days they smiled or helped you. Same person, same behavior; your belief just filtered which moments you counted.
points: You defend beliefs like a lawyer, not weigh them like a judge
Agreeable facts pass easily; disagreeable ones get extra scrutiny
It feels like open-minded research, which is why it's so sneaky
The cure: actively seek the strongest argument against yourself
chunks.key_takeaway: Once you believe something, your brain hunts for reasons you're right — not reasons you're wrong.

key_terms.definition: The tendency to seek and trust evidence that supports what you already believe, while dismissing the rest.
An environment where you mostly hear views that match your own, making them feel more certain than they are.

chunks.heading: Seeing the glitch is your new superpower
chunks.idea: You can't delete your biases — but you can learn to feel them coming. That flicker of 'wait, this feels too easy' is the whole game.
chunks.detail:
Here's the honest part. Knowing about biases doesn't switch them off. Your fast lane will keep blurting confident answers for the rest of your life.

But you gain something big: a warning light. Once you know the traps have names, you start catching that little 'this is obviously true' feeling — and learning to pause on it.

The move is small. When a conclusion feels instant and certain, especially when it flatters you or fits what you already think, that's your cue to slow down and wake the slow lane.

You don't need to double-check everything — that's exhausting and pointless. You save the effort for what matters: big money choices, strong opinions, high-stakes calls.

From here on, you'll start spotting these glitches everywhere — in ads, in arguments, in your own head. That noticing is the toolkit this whole course is built on.
chunks.example: A shopper sees '$200, now $99!' and feels a rush to buy. The trained mind pauses: 'Is $99 actually a good price, or am I just anchored to that crossed-out $200?' Same tag, but catching the pull turns a reflex into a real choice.
points: You can't erase biases — but you can notice them firing
The warning sign is a conclusion that feels instant and certain
Extra suspicion when an answer flatters you or fits your side
Spend your slow, careful thinking only where the stakes are high
chunks.key_takeaway: You'll never stop your biases from firing — but noticing them fire is the skill that changes everything.

key_terms.definition: Thinking about your own thinking — noticing how and why you reached a conclusion.
Letting a first number (like a crossed-out price) secretly set what later numbers feel reasonable.

recap: Your mind has a fast, automatic gear and a slow, effortful one — and the fast one runs the show
Biases are built-in, predictable errors that even smart people can't switch off
They come from mental shortcuts and from ancient survival wiring that misfires in the modern world
You can't delete biases, but noticing them fire — especially when an answer feels too easy — is the core skill

check.question: Why did evolution build biases into our brains if they make us wrong so often?
check.answer: Because evolution rewarded survival, not accuracy. When one kind of mistake was deadly (missing a real predator) and the other was cheap (a false alarm), a brain that leaned toward the cheap mistake lived longer — so fast, biased shortcuts got passed down even though they're often wrong.

sources.name: Daniel Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (overview)
Haselton et al., 'The Evolution of Cognitive Bias' (Wiley)

parts.next_up: Part 2: the logical traps hiding inside everyday arguments — the sneaky moves that make nonsense sound convincing, and how to name them on the spot.
parts.session_title: The lies your brain tells you

parts.recap_so_far: Last time you saw how your own brain quietly bends the truth — the built-in biases that make bad thinking feel obvious and right. Now we sharpen the other half of the toolkit: how to look at any claim and check whether it actually holds up.

chunks.heading: An argument is just reasons plus a point
chunks.idea: Forget shouting matches. In clear thinking, an "argument" is simply a claim someone wants you to believe, plus the reasons they give for it. Learn to see that shape and you can dissect anything.
chunks.detail:
When someone tries to convince you of something, they are really doing two things at once. They state a point they want you to accept. And they offer reasons meant to make you accept it.

That point is called the conclusion. The reasons are called the premises. Every real argument is premises pushing toward a conclusion.

The trick is that in normal speech these are all mashed together in one flowing sentence. Your job is to gently pull them apart and lay them side by side.

Once you see the skeleton — these reasons, therefore this point — you stop reacting to the tone and start judging the substance. That is where clear thinking begins.
chunks.example: A friend says: "You should leave for the airport now — traffic is brutal on Fridays and your flight boards in two hours." The conclusion is "leave now." The two premises are "traffic is brutal on Fridays" and "your flight boards in two hours." Same words, but now you can inspect each part.
points: An argument = premises (the reasons) + a conclusion (the point).
The conclusion is what they want you to believe.
Everyday speech hides this skeleton inside one sentence.
Pulling the parts apart is step one of judging any claim.
chunks.key_takeaway: Every argument is just reasons aimed at a point — find the point, then find the reasons.

key_terms.definition: A reason or piece of evidence offered to support a claim.
The main point the argument is trying to get you to accept.

chunks.heading: The little words that give it away
chunks.idea: You don't have to guess which part is which. Language leaves fingerprints — small signpost words that quietly announce, "a reason is coming" or "here's the point."
chunks.detail:
Some words almost always sit in front of a reason. "Because," "since," "given that," and "for" all wave a flag that says: a premise follows.

Other words sit in front of the point. "Therefore," "so," "thus," "hence," and "it follows that" all announce: here comes the conclusion.

So when you're stuck, hunt for these signpost words first. They cut through the clutter and show you the shape fast.

And when there are no signposts at all, use a simple test. Ask, "What is this person really trying to get me to believe?" That answer is the conclusion; everything supporting it is a premise.
chunks.example: "Since the bridge is closed and every side road floods in this rain, we'll be late." The word "since" flags two premises — bridge closed, roads flood — and the conclusion, "we'll be late," is what's left standing after you strip them away.
points: Premise signposts: because, since, given that, for.
Conclusion signposts: therefore, so, thus, hence, it follows.
No signposts? Ask what they're really trying to make you believe.
That answer is the conclusion; its supports are the premises.
chunks.key_takeaway: Signpost words are free directions — "because" points to a reason, "therefore" points to the point.

key_terms.definition: A signpost word like 'because' or 'therefore' that shows whether a reason or a conclusion is coming next.

chunks.heading: The reason nobody said out loud
chunks.idea: Most arguments lean on a hidden premise — an assumption so obvious to the speaker they never bother to say it. Spot the silent one and you often find where the argument is secretly weak.
chunks.detail: People rarely spell out every step. They skip the parts they assume you already share. That skipped part is a hidden premise — a reason left unspoken but still doing real work.
chunks.example: "He went to a top university, so he'll be a great manager." The stated premise is the fancy degree. The hidden premise — never said out loud — is "top universities produce great managers." Say that silent step aloud and it suddenly looks shaky, which is exactly the point.
points: A hidden premise is an unstated assumption the argument still needs.
Speakers skip what they assume you already believe.
Say the silent step out loud to test it.
Weak arguments often hide their weakest link this way.
chunks.key_takeaway: The most important part of an argument is often the reason nobody said out loud.

key_terms.definition: An unspoken assumption an argument depends on but never states directly.

chunks.heading: Does the point actually follow?
chunks.idea: Here's the first big test of any argument: even if the reasons were true, would the point really follow from them? That link — reasons to conclusion — is called validity, and it's where many arguments quietly break.
chunks.detail:
Validity is about the connection, not the content. It asks one thing: if we pretend the premises are true, are we forced to accept the conclusion? If yes, the logic is tight.

An argument can have true-sounding reasons and still fail this test. The reasons might be true and the point still not follow — the link between them is just broken.

So separate two questions in your head. One: are the reasons true? Two: does the conclusion actually follow from them? Validity is only the second question.

This is the sharpest tool in the kit. Many bad arguments pile up true facts and then leap to a conclusion those facts never supported.
chunks.example: "All cats have whiskers. My dog has whiskers. So my dog is a cat." Both premises are true, yet the conclusion is absurd — whiskers don't make something a cat. The facts are fine; the link is broken. That's an invalid argument.
points: Validity = if the premises were true, the conclusion would have to follow.
It judges the link, not whether the reasons are actually true.
True facts can still lead to a conclusion that doesn't follow.
Ask separately: are the reasons true, and does the point follow?
chunks.key_takeaway: A valid argument is one where, if the reasons hold, the conclusion cannot help but hold too.

key_terms.definition: A quality of an argument where the conclusion must be true if all the premises are true.

chunks.heading: Tight logic isn't enough — are the reasons true?
chunks.idea: An argument can have perfect logic and still be worthless if it's built on false reasons. Only when the logic is tight AND the premises are actually true do you get the gold standard: a sound argument.
chunks.detail:
Validity gets you halfway. It guarantees that if the reasons are true, the point holds. But it says nothing about whether those reasons are actually true.

So you need a second check: are the premises really, factually correct? An argument that is valid and also has true premises is called sound. That's the whole prize.

This is why you can dismantle an argument in two different ways. Attack the logic — show the point doesn't follow. Or attack a premise — show one of the reasons is false.

Strong arguments survive both attacks. Weak ones fold under at least one, and your job is simply to find which.
chunks.example: "All birds can fly. Penguins are birds. So penguins can fly." The logic is perfectly valid — if both premises were true, the conclusion would follow. But the first premise is false; not all birds fly. So the argument is valid yet unsound, and its conclusion is wrong.
points: Sound = valid logic PLUS premises that are actually true.
Validity alone can't save a false premise.
Break an argument two ways: attack the logic or attack a reason.
A truly strong argument survives both attacks.
chunks.key_takeaway: Good logic on false reasons is still a bad argument — you need soundness, not just validity.

key_terms.definition: When an argument is both valid and all of its premises are actually true.

chunks.heading: Seeing arguments everywhere now
chunks.idea: This one skill quietly upgrades everything you read and hear — ads, news, a boss's plan, a heated group chat. You stop asking "do I like this?" and start asking "do the reasons hold, and does the point follow?"
chunks.detail:
Once you have this lens, you can't turn it off. Every ad, headline, and confident opinion becomes a little puzzle: what's the conclusion, what are the premises, and where's the crack?

Most real-world arguments aren't outright lies. They're valid-looking leaps built on one shaky or unstated premise — the kind you now know to hunt for.

So build a two-question habit. First, are all the reasons actually true? Second, even if they are, does the conclusion really follow?

An argument that passes both is worth believing. One that fails either isn't — no matter how confident, loud, or polished the person delivering it sounds.
chunks.example: An ad says: "Nine out of ten dentists recommend this toothpaste, so it's the best." Premise: dentists recommend it. Conclusion: it's the best. The leap hides a shaky premise — "most-recommended" doesn't mean "best," and you don't even know what they were compared against. The polish hid a weak link.
points: Real-world arguments usually break at one shaky or hidden premise.
Two-question habit: Are the reasons true? Does the point follow?
Pass both = believe it; fail either = don't.
Confidence and polish are not evidence of a strong argument.
chunks.key_takeaway: From now on, judge claims by two questions — are the reasons true, and does the conclusion follow — never by how confident the speaker sounds.

key_terms.definition: The mental jump from the premises to the conclusion — the reasoning step you're testing.

recap: An argument is premises (reasons) supporting a conclusion (the point).
Signpost words like 'because' and 'therefore' — and hidden, unstated premises — reveal the structure.
Validity asks: if the reasons were true, would the conclusion have to follow?
Soundness asks for both — tight logic AND premises that are actually true.

check.question: An argument goes: "All fish live in water. Dolphins live in water. So dolphins are fish." The premises are both true and dolphins do live in water — yet the conclusion is false. What went wrong: the reasons, or the logic?
check.answer: The logic. The argument is invalid — living in water doesn't make something a fish, so the conclusion doesn't follow even though the premises are true. Valid logic would require a link the premises never provide.

sources.name: Informal Logic — Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy
Validity and Soundness — Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy
A Rulebook for Arguments, by Anthony Weston
Being Logical: A Guide to Good Thinking, by D. Q. McInerny

parts.next_up: Part 3: the most common ways arguments cheat — the classic logical fallacies, and how to name them the moment you see them.

parts.recap_so_far: So far you've learned how your own brain quietly bends the truth (Part 1), and how to crack any claim open into its premises and conclusion so you can test whether it actually holds (Part 2). Now we turn outward — to the tricks other people (and sometimes you) use to make a weak argument feel strong.

chunks.heading: A fallacy is a trick wearing a lab coat
chunks.idea: A logical fallacy is an argument that feels convincing but breaks the rules you learned in Part 2. It looks like reasoning. It isn't. And the moment you can name one, it loses its power over you.
chunks.detail:
Think of a fallacy as a counterfeit bill. It has the right color, the right size, the right feel in your hand. But it's fake — and once you spot the flaw, you can never un-see it.

In Part 2 you learned a good argument needs true premises that actually lead to the conclusion. A fallacy sneaks past this by hiding a broken link behind something that *feels* right — a strong emotion, a confident voice, a clever twist.

Here's the strange part: fallacies work even when they're obvious in hindsight. Smart people fall for them every single day. That's not stupidity — it's just how persuasion sneaks around logic.

The cure is almost magical in its simplicity: give the trick a name. "That's a straw man." "That's an ad hominem." Naming it snaps you out of the spell and back into clear thinking.
chunks.example: You're mid-argument and someone says, "So you basically want the whole system to collapse?" It stings, and you scramble to defend a thing you never said. But if the word "straw man" pops into your head, the panic drains away — you calmly reply, "That's not my point," and you're back in control.
points: A fallacy is an argument that persuades without actually proving anything.
They fool smart people too — falling for one isn't a sign of weakness.
Naming the trick out loud (even in your head) breaks its grip.
Every fallacy is really just a broken link between premises and conclusion.
chunks.key_takeaway: You can't defend yourself against a trick you can't name — so this whole part is about giving the tricks their names.

key_terms.definition: A flaw in reasoning that makes an argument feel strong even though it doesn't actually prove its point.
The art of persuasion — using language to move people, which can be honest or sneaky.

chunks.heading: Ad hominem: attack the person, dodge the point
chunks.idea: The oldest trick in the book: instead of answering what someone said, you attack who they are. It feels like a win. It proves nothing.
chunks.detail:
"Ad hominem" is Latin for "to the person." It means aiming your fire at the speaker instead of their argument.

Here's why it's a trick: a claim is either true or false on its own. Who says it doesn't change that. Even a person you dislike can be right, and even a genius can be wrong.

The sneaky version is subtle. It's not always name-calling. Sometimes it's "Well, of course *you'd* say that" — quietly implying the person is too biased to bother listening to.

Watch for the switch. A real rebuttal engages the idea. An ad hominem quietly changes the subject from the idea to the human holding it, and hopes you don't notice the swap.
chunks.example: A doctor argues that a new diet is unhealthy. Instead of checking her evidence, someone snaps, "She's overweight herself — why should we listen?" But her weight has nothing to do with whether her data on the diet is correct. The argument still stands, untouched.
points: Ad hominem = attacking the person instead of their argument.
A claim's truth doesn't depend on who says it.
The subtle form is "of course *you'd* say that" — dismissing by identity, not logic.
Ask: did they answer the point, or just insult the person making it?
chunks.key_takeaway: The messenger's flaws don't make the message false — deal with the idea, not the identity.

key_terms.definition: Attacking the person making an argument instead of engaging with the argument itself.

chunks.heading: The straw man: fight a fake version
chunks.idea: You build a flimsy, exaggerated version of what someone said — a scarecrow — then triumphantly knock it down. The crowd cheers. But you never touched their real point.
chunks.detail:
Picture a scarecrow made of straw. It's easy to punch over. That's exactly the point: you replace a real, sturdy argument with a weak imitation you can easily beat.

It usually starts with "So what you're saying is..." followed by a wild exaggeration of the original claim. The distortion does all the work.

Why is it so effective? Because to onlookers it *looks* like you won. You knocked something down, and it fell over. They rarely check whether it was the real thing you knocked down.

The honest opposite is called "steel-manning" — repeating someone's argument in its *strongest* form before you reply. If you can beat the best version, you've really won something.
chunks.example: Person A says, "I think we should have some speed limits near schools." Person B replies, "So you want the government controlling every move we make on the road?" A never said that. B invented an extreme version — the straw man — because it's far easier to attack than the reasonable thing actually said.
points: Straw man = distorting an argument into a weaker version, then attacking that.
Tell-tale opener: "So what you're *really* saying is..." followed by an exaggeration.
It looks like a win to bystanders even though nothing real was refuted.
The antidote is steel-manning: attack the strongest version, not the weakest.
chunks.key_takeaway: If you have to exaggerate someone's point to beat it, you haven't beaten their point at all.

key_terms.definition: Misrepresenting someone's argument as a weaker or extreme version so it's easier to attack.
Restating an opponent's argument in its strongest, fairest form before responding to it.

chunks.heading: The false choice and the runaway slope
chunks.idea: Two tricks that mess with structure. One shrinks your options to a fake either/or. The other pretends one small step must tumble into disaster. Both hide the real range of what's possible.
chunks.detail:
The first is the **false dilemma**: "You're either with us or against us." It squeezes a rich, many-shaded question down to just two boxes — usually one scary, one obvious. Real life almost always has a third door, and a fourth.

The trick works by hiding the middle. Once you notice options are missing, the forced choice falls apart. Ask yourself: are these *really* the only two roads?

The second is the **slippery slope**: "If we allow this small thing, next it'll be a bigger thing, and soon everything collapses." It treats a whole chain of maybes as if each step is guaranteed.

Sometimes slopes are real — some actions do lead to others. But it's a fallacy when no evidence is given for *why* one step must force the next. A scary story about the bottom isn't proof you'll slide there.
chunks.example: "If we let kids use calculators in class, next they won't learn any math, and eventually no one will be able to add at all." Each leap is just assumed, not shown. Meanwhile, "Support this bill or you don't care about safety" is a false dilemma — as if caring about safety and disliking one specific bill can't both be true.
points: False dilemma = only two options offered when more exist. Look for the hidden third door.
Slippery slope = claiming one step must trigger a chain to disaster, with no proof of the links.
Both work by making reality look narrower or more inevitable than it is.
Defense: ask "Are those really the only choices?" and "Why must this step force the next?"
chunks.key_takeaway: Real questions rarely have exactly two answers, and one small step rarely guarantees the fall — be suspicious of anything that pretends otherwise.

key_terms.definition: Presenting only two options when more actually exist, forcing an artificial either/or choice.
Claiming one small step will inevitably lead to an extreme outcome, without showing the steps are actually linked.

chunks.heading: The emotional levers: fear, fame, and the crowd
chunks.idea: Some tricks skip your logic entirely and pull on your feelings instead. Three big levers: your fear, your respect for a famous name, and your urge to follow the crowd.
chunks.detail:
The **appeal to emotion** wins you over with a feeling instead of a reason. A charity ad with sad music, a politician warning of danger — the goal is to make you *feel* so strongly you stop asking whether the argument is actually sound.

Feelings aren't the enemy. The trick is when emotion *replaces* evidence rather than joining it. Ask: strip away the feeling — is there still a real reason here?

The **appeal to authority** says "an expert (or celebrity) said it, so it's true." Experts are genuinely useful — but only inside their field, and only when the wider evidence agrees. A famous actor selling a health product is not proof of anything.

The **bandwagon** says "everyone believes it, so it must be right." But popularity isn't truth. Millions of people once believed the sun orbited the Earth. The crowd can be confidently, completely wrong.
chunks.example: "Nine out of ten people switched to this brand — don't get left behind!" That's the bandwagon and a nudge of fear in one line. Notice it gives you zero information about whether the product is actually good. It just makes standing still feel lonely.
points: Appeal to emotion = using feelings to replace evidence, not support it.
Appeal to authority = 'an expert/celebrity said so' — only valid in their field, backed by real evidence.
Bandwagon = 'everyone believes it, so it's true' — but popularity isn't proof.
For each, ask: what's left if I remove the feeling, the famous name, or the crowd?
chunks.key_takeaway: Fear, fame, and the crowd can all be loud — but loud is not the same as true.

key_terms.definition: Trying to win an argument by stirring feelings instead of giving reasons.
Treating a claim as true just because a famous or powerful person said it, rather than because of evidence.
Arguing something is true or right simply because many people believe or do it.

chunks.heading: Now you'll see them everywhere
chunks.idea: Here's the gift and the curse of this part: once you can name these tricks, you can't stop noticing them — in ads, in politics, in arguments with friends, and in your own mouth.
chunks.detail:
Turn on any debate, any commercial, any comment thread, and the fallacies light up like neon signs. The ad hominem in the political jab. The bandwagon in the ad. The false dilemma in the ultimatum.

But the real power move is turning the lens on yourself. When you're losing an argument, notice the urge to reach for a straw man or a cheap insult. Catching your *own* tricks is where clear thinking really begins.

A warning, though: don't become the person who yells "Fallacy!" to dodge every point. Sometimes a flawed argument still has a true conclusion — a bad reason doesn't automatically make someone wrong. Naming the trick means the *argument* fails, not that the *claim* is false.

So hold this lightly. The goal isn't to win fights. It's to see clearly — to tell the difference between something that's actually true and something that's just cleverly dressed to look true.
chunks.example: You spot a friend using a slippery slope: "If I skip the gym today, I'll never go again." Instead of smugly announcing the fallacy, you just gently ask, "Is one skipped day really the same as quitting?" You've used the skill to think more clearly together — not to score a point.
points: Once named, these tricks become impossible to un-see in daily life.
The hardest and most valuable target is your own reasoning.
A fallacy means the *argument* is broken — the conclusion could still happen to be true.
Use this to see clearly, not to 'win' by shouting 'Fallacy!' at everything.
chunks.key_takeaway: Spotting the trick isn't about winning arguments — it's about seeing the difference between what's true and what's merely convincing.

key_terms.definition: The mistake of assuming that because an argument contains a fallacy, its conclusion must be false.

recap: A fallacy is an argument that feels convincing but breaks the rules — and naming it is what breaks its spell.
Watch for the person-attacks: ad hominem (insult the speaker) and straw man (beat a fake version of their point).
Watch for the structure-benders: false dilemma (fake either/or) and slippery slope (a chain of unproven maybes).
Watch for the emotional levers: appeal to emotion, appeal to authority, and bandwagon — loud, but not proof.
A broken argument doesn't make the conclusion false — use these tools to see clearly, not just to win.

check.question: Your friend says, "That climate scientist drives a big car, so his warnings about pollution are nonsense." Which fallacy is this, and why doesn't it work?
check.answer: It's an ad hominem — attacking the person instead of the argument. The scientist's car habits have nothing to do with whether his data on pollution is correct; the claim has to be judged on its own evidence, not on the speaker's lifestyle.

sources.name: Fallacies — Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Bradley Dowden)
Logical Fallacies — Purdue Online Writing Lab (OWL)
An Illustrated Book of Bad Arguments — Ali Almossawi

parts.next_up: Part 4: 'Follow the evidence' — how to tell a real reason from a fake one, why correlation isn't cause, and how to weigh proof like a detective instead of a fan.

parts.recap_so_far: So far you've learned that your brain quietly bends the truth (Part 1), how to split any claim into premises and a conclusion (Part 2), and how to name the sneaky tricks and fallacies people use (Part 3). Now comes the part that decides who's actually right — what really counts as evidence.

chunks.heading: Evidence is anything that should change your mind
chunks.idea: Not every reason is evidence. Real evidence is a fact that, if true, makes a claim more likely — and would make it less likely if the fact pointed the other way. A story that fits your belief no matter what proves nothing.
chunks.detail:
Think of evidence as a weight you drop on one side of a scale. Good evidence tips the scale. If a fact would look exactly the same whether the claim is true or false, it's not really weighing anything.

Here's the test. Ask: "If this claim were WRONG, would I still see this?" If the answer is yes, the fact isn't evidence for the claim — it just feels like it.

A single dramatic story is the weakest kind. It's called an anecdote, and there's a good one for almost any claim you can imagine. "My grandpa smoked till he was 90" is a real story, but it can't outweigh millions of measured cases.

So before you argue about whether evidence is good, ask the earlier question: is this even evidence at all, or just a vivid feeling wearing a fact's clothing?
chunks.example: Someone says a lucky charm helped their team win. You ask: on the days they carried it and LOST, did they still believe in it? If yes — and they did carry it on losing days too — then wins and losses look identical whether the charm works or not. The charm predicts nothing, so it's not evidence.
points: Evidence should move your confidence up OR down — a fact that fits every outcome is empty
The key test: 'If the claim were false, would I still see this?'
One vivid story (an anecdote) is the weakest evidence — there's one for every claim
chunks.key_takeaway: If a fact would look the same whether the claim is true or false, it isn't evidence — it's decoration.

key_terms.definition: A fact that makes a claim more likely to be true — and would have made it less likely if it pointed the other way.
A single personal story used as proof; vivid but easily found for almost any claim, so it carries little weight.

chunks.heading: Two things moving together isn't one causing the other
chunks.idea: When two things rise and fall in step, we call it correlation. It's tempting to say one causes the other — but they might just be dancing to the same hidden tune. This is the single most common reasoning mistake you'll meet.
chunks.detail:
Picture a graph where ice-cream sales and drownings both climb in the same months. It looks like ice cream is dangerous. It isn't.

The real culprit is hidden off to the side: hot weather. Heat makes people buy more ice cream AND swim more, so both numbers rise together. Neither one is causing the other.

That hidden cause has a name — a confounder, or lurking variable. Whenever two things move together, always ask: is there a third thing pulling both strings?

There are really three possibilities to keep in mind. A causes B, B causes A, or some C causes both. Correlation alone can't tell you which — and sometimes it's just pure coincidence.
chunks.example: Studies have shown that towns with more churches also have more crime. Do churches cause crime? No — bigger towns simply have more of everything: more churches AND more crime. Population is the confounder pulling both numbers up at once.
points: Correlation = two things move together; causation = one actually makes the other happen
Always hunt for the confounder — a hidden third factor driving both
Four options for any correlation: A→B, B→A, C→both, or dumb luck
chunks.key_takeaway: Correlation is a clue, never a verdict — before believing X causes Y, rule out the hidden third thing.

key_terms.definition: When two things tend to rise and fall together, without proving one causes the other.
A hidden third factor that influences both things you're comparing, creating a fake link between them.

chunks.heading: How to tell if X really causes Y
chunks.idea: If correlation can't prove cause, what can? There are a handful of signs that push a link from 'maybe coincidence' toward 'probably real.' Learn them and you'll read every 'study finds' headline differently.
chunks.detail:
The gold standard is a controlled experiment. You split people into two groups at random, change one thing for one group, and keep everything else the same. If only the changed group shifts, that one change is likely the cause.

Randomizing is the magic trick. It scatters all the hidden confounders evenly across both groups, so they cancel out and can't fool you.

When you can't run an experiment, look for other clues. Does the cause come BEFORE the effect in time? Does more of the cause give more of the effect? Is there a believable story for HOW it would work?

And the strongest sign of all: does the result show up again when other people repeat the test? A finding that only ever happens once, in one lab, is a whisper — not a fact.
chunks.example: To learn if a drug lowers blood pressure, you don't just watch people who took it. You randomly give half the patients the real pill and half a fake sugar pill, and nobody knows which. If the real-pill group drops more, the drug is the likely cause — because randomizing made the two groups otherwise identical.
points: Randomized experiments beat mere correlation because they cancel out hidden confounders
Cause should come before effect, and more cause should mean more effect
A finding that others can reproduce is far stronger than a one-time result
chunks.key_takeaway: The closest thing to proof of cause is a randomized experiment that other people can repeat and get the same answer.

key_terms.definition: A test where you change one thing and hold everything else steady, so any difference in the result can be traced to that one thing.
Assigning people to groups by chance, which spreads hidden factors evenly so they can't secretly skew the result.

chunks.heading: Small samples lie confidently
chunks.idea: How many cases a claim rests on matters enormously. A pattern seen in 5 people can be pure luck; the same pattern in 5,000 is hard to fake. We badly underrate this — our brains treat tiny samples as if they were the whole truth.
chunks.detail:
Flip a fair coin four times and getting three heads is no shock. You might wrongly decide the coin is 'lucky.' Flip it four hundred times and three-quarters heads would be almost impossible — the truth has room to show itself.

This is why small samples swing wildly. With few cases, random noise dominates; the average bounces all over the place. With many cases, the noise cancels out and the real signal settles in.

Psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky called our blind spot here the 'law of small numbers' — our habit of trusting a tiny sample as if it spoke for everyone. Even trained scientists fall for it.

So when you hear a striking claim, ask the boring question first: how many? A '90% of people preferred it' means little if 'people' was ten friends of the seller.
chunks.example: A tiny village posts the country's LOWEST cancer rate, so people call it healthy. But the same tiny villages also post the HIGHEST rates. Why? With only 200 residents, one or two cases swings the percentage enormously. The size, not the lifestyle, is doing the talking.
points: Small samples are dominated by luck; big samples let the real pattern show
Always ask 'how many cases?' before trusting a percentage or a trend
The 'law of small numbers': we wrongly expect tiny samples to represent everyone
chunks.key_takeaway: A striking result from a tiny sample is usually just noise wearing a costume — ask 'how many?' every single time.

key_terms.definition: How many cases or people a finding is based on; bigger samples give more trustworthy answers.
The mistake of believing a small sample reliably represents the whole group, when it's actually ruled by chance.

chunks.heading: Weighing a claim honestly
chunks.idea: Putting it together: judging a claim isn't yes-or-no, it's a scale you load with weights. The honest thinker asks how strong each piece is — and, crucially, uses the same standard whether the claim flatters their side or not.
chunks.detail:
Start with how surprising the claim is. Extraordinary claims need extraordinary evidence — a friend saying it rained needs almost none; a friend saying they saw a ghost needs a lot.

Then weigh the evidence by quality, not by how loud it is. A big randomized study outweighs a viral anecdote, even though the anecdote feels more real because it has a face and a story.

Now the hardest part — the honesty test. Ask: 'Would I accept this same evidence if it argued AGAINST what I already believe?' If you'd wave it away then, you shouldn't lean on it now.

Finally, don't demand certainty. Good thinking means holding claims at the confidence the evidence earns — strong, likely, unsure — and being willing to move that dial when new evidence arrives.
chunks.example: Two headlines drop. 'New study of 40,000 people links a habit to heart risk' and 'Influencer says the habit cured her overnight.' The first is a heavy weight on the scale; the second is a feather with a loud voice. If you'd trust the big study when it agreed with you, honesty means trusting it when it doesn't.
points: Extraordinary claims demand extraordinary evidence — scale your skepticism to the surprise
The honesty test: apply the same standard to evidence you like and evidence you don't
Aim for calibrated confidence, not certainty — and update when better evidence arrives
chunks.key_takeaway: You reason honestly only when you judge evidence by the same rules whether it supports your side or attacks it.

key_terms.definition: Claims that would overturn a lot of what we know; they require unusually strong evidence before we should believe them.
Holding a belief exactly as strongly as the evidence justifies — no more, no less — and adjusting as evidence changes.

recap: Evidence is only evidence if it would look different when the claim is false — a fact that fits any outcome proves nothing.
Correlation isn't causation: two things moving together may share a hidden third cause (a confounder), so hunt for it before believing one causes the other.
Sample size rules everything — tiny samples are ruled by luck, so always ask 'how many?' before trusting a pattern.
Weigh claims honestly: scale skepticism to how surprising the claim is, judge evidence by quality not volume, and use the same standard for facts you like and dislike.

check.question: A city notices that months with more ice-cream sales also have more drownings, and concludes ice cream is dangerous. What's the reasoning error, and what's really going on?
check.answer: It confuses correlation with causation. Ice cream doesn't cause drowning — a hidden third factor, hot summer weather (a confounder), drives both up at the same time, because heat makes people buy more ice cream and also swim more.

sources.name: Correlation does not imply causation — Wikipedia
The Law of Small Numbers — Effectiviology
Thinking, Fast and Slow — Daniel Kahneman (book)
The Demon-Haunted World: Science as a Candle in the Dark (the 'Baloney Detection Kit') — Carl Sagan (book)

parts.next_up: Part 5: Changing your mind — how the best thinkers update their beliefs when the facts shift, and why being wrong on purpose is a superpower.
